quote: He called twelve and gave them authority over unclean spirits.
firstReading: Hosea condemns Israel for prosperity without fidelity: their luxuriant growth has produced arrogance and idolatry, and their political and religious leaders lead the people astray. The prophet warns of coming judgment—destruction and exile—because of reliance on alliances and false gods. Yet Hosea issues a call to repentance: "Sow for yourselves righteousness, reap steadfast love," urging the people to break up their fallow ground and return to covenant faithfulness, so that healing and restoration might follow.
psalmSummary: Psalm 105 invites the faithful to praise and seek the Lord actively: remember his wondrous deeds, rejoice in his name, and search for his presence. The verses call God’s people to recount his marvelous works and the covenant given to Abraham, the founder of Israel. It is a communal summons to keep faith alive by recalling God’s acts of mercy and fidelity, thereby sustaining trust and obedience across generations.
gospelSummary: In Matthew 10:1-7 Jesus summons the twelve apostles, empowering them with authority to cast out unclean spirits and to heal every disease and infirmity. He commissions them to proclaim the nearness of God’s kingdom and sends them primarily to the lost sheep of Israel. Jesus gives direct instructions for mission: rely on hospitality, avoid unnecessary contact with Gentiles and Samaritans in this initial sending, and focus on announcing that the kingdom of heaven has come near, demonstrating it through word and deed.
saintReflection: Saint Gregory Grassi and Companions (Saint of the Day): Saint Gregory Grassi and his companions were among the martyrs of China whose lives joined missionary service to the ultimate witness of blood. Bishops, priests, religious, and lay faithful stood together in fidelity during persecution. Their communion in suffering shows that the Gospel creates one family across cultures, and that mission is sustained by courage, patience, and hope in the risen Lord.
dailyPrayer: Lord Jesus, you called the twelve and sent them forth with authority to heal and to proclaim your kingdom. Give us the courage to hear that same call today: to uproot what resists your grace, to sow righteousness in our families and communities, and to serve those in need. Strengthen missionaries and all who endure persecution; grant them perseverance and peace. Inspire our hearts to seek you with gladness, to remember your wondrous deeds, and to live in covenant faithfulness. Through the intercession of Saint Gregory Grassi and his companions, may we bear witness to your love with patience, humility, and hope. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: The readings form a tight moral-theological arc: Hosea confronts the perils of nominal prosperity—religious forms without covenant fidelity—and insists on interior conversion: "break up your fallow ground" and sow righteousness. The psalm grounds the community’s memory in God’s saving acts, urging praise and active seeking of the Lord. Matthew’s Gospel answers with pastoral commissioning: the authority given to the twelve manifests God’s restorative in-breaking in word and deed. Together these texts teach that authentic worship prompts mission, and mission is both sign and instrument of God’s covenant fidelity. The martyrs—Gregory Grassi and companions—illustrate the Gospel’s ultimate cost and promise: witness borne in suffering testifies to the kingdom’s power and calls the community to courageous, sacrificial fidelity.
exegesis:
Context: Hosea speaks in a period of political fragility and social excess. Northern Israel has prospered materially but has abandoned covenant commitments; worship has become syncretistic and hollow. Hosea’s prophetic rhetoric turns agricultural imagery into moral summons: luxuriant growth that should signal blessing instead exposes decadence and spiritual barrenness. The prophet anticipates judgment—utter ruin for misplaced trust in alliances and idols—yet his tone is not only denunciatory; embedded in the critique is an invitation to repentance and renewed covenant life.

Psalm: The chosen verses of Psalm 105 function as catechesis in communal memory. By calling the people to 'seek the Lord' and 'remember his wondrous deeds,' the psalm frames identity as recollection of God’s past fidelity. For Israel, history is theological: remembering shapes present obedience. The psalm’s appeal to praise and to seeking God underscores that liturgical and ethical life belong together; praise fuels mission and faithful memory corrects complacency when prosperity tempts faithlessness.

Gospel: Matthew 10:1-7 launches the apostolic mission with two complementary emphases: authority and direction. Authority over unclean spirits and disease signals the kingdom’s restorative power; the apostles act as Christ’s delegated agents. But authority is not absolutist; it is ordered to proclamation and reliance on others. Jesus’ instruction to go to 'the lost sheep of Israel' situates the first phase of mission within covenant history, respecting God’s prior promises while preparing the ground for later universal outreach.

Saints: The witness of Saint Gregory Grassi and companions shows how the dynamics of these texts play out in concrete history. Missionary service brought them into contact with cross-cultural challenges and ultimately persecution. Their communion in suffering—bishops, priests, religious, laity together—reality-tests the claim that the Gospel forms a family across cultural boundaries. Martyrdom reframes authority: the apostles’ power to heal and exorcize foreshadows a truer power manifested in self-giving, fidelity unto death.

Today: The prophetic indictment of Hosea remains urgent for communities that equate material success with divine favor. 'Sowing righteousness' calls for structural and personal conversion: dismantling idols of wealth, prestige, or ethnic privilege and cultivating justice and mercy. The psalm’s call to remember God’s deeds resists spiritual amnesia; recollection fosters gratitude and responsibility. The Gospel’s commissioning models mission that is both authoritative and dependent—authority exercised in service, mission carried out in dependence on providence and hospitality.

Pastoral Application: Pastors and believers must integrate prophecy, praise, and mission. Practically, this means forming hearts through liturgy and scripture memory, converting economic and social practices to serve the poor, and supporting missionaries whose ministry risks cost. The martyrs’ witness invites communities to value solidarity over comfort and to pray for persecuted Christians. Ecclesially, the texts urge prudence: mission begins with faithful cultivation of home communities and proceeds outward, propelled by the Holy Spirit and sustained by a people formed in praise and repentance.

Conclusion: The sequence of readings presents a coherent summons: repent where prosperity has corroded faith, remember and praise God’s deeds, and then go forth empowered to embody the kingdom. Authority in Christ is entrusted to those who will serve and, when necessary, witness unto death. The challenge and consolation are twin: the Gospel calls us to costly fidelity, and God’s saving acts—remembered and proclaimed—equip communities to answer that call with courage and hope.
